title: Prologue
theme: Entry — crowds and the human collective
notes: Entrance archway with crowd imagery per MoMA archive-highlights page; Sandburg's prologue (distributed in full as a leaflet) opens the exhibition. Thematic cluster reconstructed from MoMA institutional records.

title: Lovers
notes: Thematic cluster attested in MoMA institutional summaries and CNA education portal (love); sequencing reconstructed from MoMA archive-highlights page.

title: Marriage and childbirth
notes: CNA education portal names childbirth among the 37 themes; childbirth is attested in MoMA archive-highlights as following lovers. Thematic cluster, not a verbatim catalog heading.

title: Family and children
notes: CNA portal lists family and childhood among the themes; MoMA archive-highlights describes household life after childbirth.

title: Play, learning, and education
notes: CNA portal names education among the themes. Grouping of play and learning reconstructed from secondary summaries.

title: Work
notes: MoMA archive-highlights names careers; CNA names work; Barthes 1957 explicitly treats work as one of the exhibition's universalized categories.

title: Eating and everyday life
notes: Eating together is widely recognized as a recurring motif in the exhibition's mid-flow sequence. Thematic cluster reconstructed from catalog sequencing.

title: Relationships and community
notes: Mid-exhibition social-life grouping. Thematic cluster reconstructed from catalog sequencing; exact boundaries are not canonical.

title: Hardship, suffering, and war
notes: CNA lists war among the themes; the exhibition famously included a large H-bomb image; Barthes 1957 critiques the exhibition's handling of suffering as ahistorical.

title: Death and mourning
notes: Attested in MoMA archive-highlights (Death) and CNA/Sandeen analyses; Barthes 1957 directly discusses the exhibition's treatment of death as a naturalized universal.

title: Rededication, peace, and the future
theme: New life; the child, democracy, peace
notes: CNA lists peace among the themes; the exhibition closes with an image of children (W. Eugene Smith, A Walk to Paradise Garden, 1946) per MoMA archive-highlights. Thematic cluster for closing sequence.
